Entries.Name: Account Protection Warning
Entries.Detail: Windows Security shows a warning if you are not signed in with a Microsoft account. Disabling hides this recurring notification.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Account Protection warning is shown
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Hides the recurring Windows Security nag to sign into a Microsoft account. Security posture is unchanged; this only removes the notification.

Entries.Name: Apps SmartScreen
Entries.Description: SmartScreen marks downloaded files as unsafe
Entries.Detail: SmartScreen checks downloaded files and apps against a cloud reputation database and warns you before running unknown executables.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: SmartScreen checks for apps and files are enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: When you run an unfamiliar executable, SmartScreen checks its reputation against Microsoft's cloud database and blocks or warns if it is unknown or flagged. Disabling means no reputation check runs before execution.

Entries.Name: Boot Recovery
Entries.Detail: Controls whether Windows automatically enters recovery mode after consecutive failed boot attempts.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Automatic boot recovery mode is enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Without automatic recovery, a machine that fails to boot consecutively will stay at the boot menu rather than entering WinRE automatically. Useful on headless systems where WinRE loops are harder to interrupt.

Entries.Name: Core Isolation Memory Integrity
Entries.Detail: Hypervisor-protected code integrity runs Windows kernel code in a virtualized environment to prevent kernel-level malware and rootkits.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Core Isolation Memory Integrity (HVCI) is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Runs kernel code integrity checks inside a hypervisor-protected environment so kernel-level malware cannot tamper with driver signing verification. Incompatible drivers will be blocked from loading after enabling.

Entries.Name: Defender Application Guard
Entries.Detail: Opens Edge browser tabs in an isolated virtual machine to prevent malicious websites from accessing the host system or its files.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Defender Application Guard is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Runs untrusted Edge tabs inside an isolated VM so a compromised tab cannot read host files or credentials. Requires hardware virtualization and adds per-tab startup latency.

Entries.Name: Defender Cloud
Entries.Detail: Enables Microsoft Defender's cloud-based protection for real-time threat lookups using Microsoft's global threat intelligence database.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Defender cloud protection is enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Submits unknown files to Microsoft's threat intelligence backend for real-time analysis. Without it, Defender relies only on local signatures and heuristics, which can miss novel malware until the next definition update.

Entries.Name: Defender Exploit Guard Policy
Entries.Description: Configure additional Defender Exploit Guard policies
Entries.Detail: Applies additional Exploit Guard attack surface reduction rules via Windows Defender to block common malware techniques.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Applies Exploit Guard attack surface reduction rules that can block legitimate applications, Office automation, admin scripts, PowerShell tooling, and line-of-business workflows.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Applies a set of Attack Surface Reduction rules that block common malware execution patterns — macro abuse, Office child processes, script injection, and others. Individual rules can be audited or excluded if they conflict with legitimate software.

Entries.Name: Defender Sandbox
Entries.Detail: Runs the Microsoft Defender antivirus engine inside an isolated sandbox process for extra protection. Adds a small performance overhead.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Defender sandboxing is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Runs the Defender scanning engine in an isolated process so a vulnerability in the scanner itself cannot be exploited to reach the rest of the OS. Adds a modest CPU overhead.

Entries.Name: Defender Tray Icon
Entries.Detail: Shows or hides the Windows Security (Defender) icon in the system tray. Hiding it does not disable Defender protection.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Defender system tray icon is shown
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Cosmetic only — removing the tray icon does not disable or weaken Defender protection.

Entries.Name: DEP OptOut
Entries.Detail: Sets Data Execution Prevention to OptOut mode, enabling DEP protection for all processes except those you explicitly exclude.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: DEP set to OptIn mode
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Enables Data Execution Prevention for every process on the system except those you explicitly exempt. OptOut means DEP is on by default and you opt individual programs out rather than having to opt them in.

Entries.Name: Dismiss MS Account Offer
Entries.Description: Dismiss Defender MS account sign-in offer
Entries.Detail: Removes the recurring prompt in Windows Security asking you to sign in with a Microsoft account for additional protection.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Defender MS account sign-in offer shown
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Suppresses the persistent Windows Security prompt to link a Microsoft account. Does not affect authentication or security policy.

Entries.Name: Dismiss SmartScreen Filter Offer
Entries.Detail: Removes the recurring prompt in Windows Security asking you to enable the SmartScreen reputation filter.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Defender SmartScreen filter offer shown
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Suppresses the persistent Windows Security prompt to turn on SmartScreen. Does not change whether SmartScreen is actually enabled.

Entries.Name: DNS over HTTPS
Entries.Detail: Encrypts DNS lookups by sending them over HTTPS instead of plain UDP. Choose a provider preset or call the function directly with a custom known DNS-over-HTTPS server pair.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: DNS-over-HTTPS is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Routes DNS queries over an encrypted HTTPS connection instead of plaintext UDP port 53. Prevents ISPs and network observers from reading your lookup traffic. Requires a DoH-capable resolver — choose a preset or enter a custom server URL.

Entries.Name: Download File Blocking
Entries.Detail: Controls whether Windows Security marks downloaded files as potentially unsafe using the Zone.Identifier alternate data stream.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Blocking of downloaded files is enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Controls whether Windows stamps downloaded files with a Zone.Identifier ADS marking them as coming from the internet. Removing this mark bypasses 'Open file – security warning' prompts but also silences warnings on genuinely risky files.

Entries.Name: Event Viewer Custom View
Entries.Description: Process Creation custom view in Event Viewer
Entries.Detail: Creates a custom view in Event Viewer filtered to show process creation events, making it easier to audit what programs have been launched.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Process Creation custom view not present in Event Viewer
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Registers a pre-built Event Viewer filter scoped to process creation events. Useful when investigating what ran on a machine without writing a custom query.

Entries.Name: F8 Boot Menu
Entries.Detail: Enables the legacy F8 key boot menu that lets you select Safe Mode and other startup options during the Windows boot process.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Legacy F8 boot menu is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Restores the pre-Windows 8 behavior where tapping F8 during POST opens the advanced boot menu. Modern UEFI fast boot makes this menu unreachable without explicitly re-enabling it.

Entries.Name: Firewall
Entries.Detail: Enables or disables Windows Defender Firewall across all network profiles. Disabling leaves all inbound ports completely unfiltered.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Firewall is enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Windows Defender Firewall applies to all network profiles. Disabling it leaves every listening port exposed with no inbound filtering. Only disable if a third-party firewall is running in its place.

Entries.Name: Import Exploit Protection Policy
Entries.Detail: Downloads and imports a pre-built exploit mitigation policy file into Windows Defender Exploit Guard, applying custom process mitigations.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Imports a pre-built exploit mitigation policy that enforces DEP, ASLR, CFG, and other protections per-process. May cause application crashes or compatibility failures in apps that are not mitigation-aware.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Downloads and imports a community-maintained exploit mitigation policy file that configures per-process mitigations beyond the system defaults. Policies may need adjustment for software that conflicts with specific mitigations.

Entries.Name: Local Security Authority Protection
Entries.Description: LSA protection to prevent code injection
Entries.Detail: Runs the Windows LSA process in protected mode to prevent credential theft by tools like Mimikatz. Recommended for all systems.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: LSA protection is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Runs the LSA process in isolated protected mode so credential-dumping tools like Mimikatz cannot read LSASS memory directly. Recommended on any system where credential theft is a realistic concern.

Entries.Name: LOLBin Firewall Rules
Entries.Description: Configure LOLBin outbound firewall block rules
Entries.Detail: Adds outbound firewall block rules for built-in Windows binaries commonly abused by malware (LOLBins) to phone home or download payloads.
Entries.CautionReason: May break VPN or enterprise connectivity. Blocks outbound network access for LOLBins (certutil, mshta, wscript, cscript, etc.). May break administrative scripts, installers, certificate enrollment, troubleshooting tools, and enterprise deployment workflows that use these binaries.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Adds outbound block rules for built-in Windows binaries routinely abused to download payloads or phone home — certutil, bitsadmin, mshta, and similar. Verify that none of these binaries are required by your legitimate automation before applying.

Entries.Name: Network Protection
Entries.Detail: Microsoft Defender's network protection blocks connections to known malicious domains and IP addresses at the network level.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Defender Exploit Guard network protection is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Blocks connections to known malicious domains and IPs at the network driver level using Defender's threat intelligence. Operates system-wide, not just within a browser.

Entries.Name: PowerShell Module Logging
Entries.Detail: Logs every command run by PowerShell modules to the Windows event log. Useful for security auditing and investigating incidents.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: PowerShell module logging is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Writes every command executed by PowerShell modules to the Windows event log (Event ID 4103). Expands visibility for incident response but can produce high log volume on systems that run automation.

Entries.Name: PowerShell Script Logging
Entries.Detail: Logs the full content of every PowerShell script block that executes to the Windows event log. Helps with post-incident investigations.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: PowerShell script logging is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Captures the full decoded content of every script block PowerShell executes (Event ID 4104), including dynamically constructed code. Essential for post-incident forensics; volume scales with script activity.

Entries.Name: PUA Detection
Entries.Detail: Enables Defender to detect and quarantine potentially unwanted applications such as adware, bundleware, and browser hijackers.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Potentially unwanted app detection is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Tells Defender to flag and quarantine potentially unwanted applications — adware, bundleware, browser hijackers, and coin miners. Can produce false positives on gray-area utilities like system cleaners.

Entries.Name: Save Zone Information
Entries.Detail: When you download a file from the internet, Windows tags it with a zone marker. Keeping this enabled allows security warnings when running downloaded files.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Downloaded files are marked as unsafe by SmartScreen
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Keeps the Zone.Identifier ADS on downloaded files so Windows can show security prompts before running them. Removing this mark means no zone-based warning will appear even for files downloaded from the internet.

Entries.Name: Sharing Mapped Drives
Entries.Detail: Allows mapped network drives to be visible to elevated (administrator) processes as well as the current logged-in user.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Sharing mapped drives between users is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: When disabled (the UAC default), elevated processes see drive letters differently from the user session, which can break scripts that assume mapped drives are visible under elevation. Enabling this makes them consistent.

Entries.Name: Windows Firewall Logging
Entries.Detail: Enables logging of dropped and allowed connections to the Windows Firewall log file so you can audit network activity.
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Writes allowed and dropped connection records to the Windows Firewall log file. Provides a network-level audit trail without requiring a full packet capture.

Entries.Name: Windows Sandbox
Entries.Detail: A lightweight isolated virtual machine for safely running untrusted applications without risking your main system. Requires Windows Pro or higher.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Sandbox is disabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Provisions a lightweight throwaway VM you can spin up on demand for testing untrusted software. Requires Windows Pro or higher and hardware virtualization. Everything inside is discarded when the sandbox window closes.

Entries.Name: Windows Script Host
Entries.Detail: Controls whether Windows can run .vbs and .js script files. Disabling prevents these script types from executing when double-clicked.
Entries.WinDefaultDesc: Windows Script Host (.js/.vbs execution) is enabled
Entries.WhyThisMatters: Controls execution of .vbs and .js script files via wscript.exe and cscript.exe. Disabling blocks these script types from running when double-clicked or invoked without a full interpreter path — a common malware delivery path.
